implementation.scheduler_strategy: The NIF is declared with #[rustler::nif(schedule = "DirtyCpu")], so policy execution does not run on normal BEAM schedulers. The spike also rejects oversized policy/context payloads, all while loops, and range() loops before invoking the Starlark evaluator.
implementation.sandbox_strategy: No loader is installed and Globals::standard is used. The Elixir/Rust surface injects only the JSON-compatible ctx value. The spike also fail-closes on obvious load/import/filesystem/network source attempts before evaluation.
implementation.trace_strategy: Trace entries include stable policy id, rule id, selected action, result, and reason. Source spans were not added in the timebox.

required_scenarios.fail_closed_on_runaway_loop: passed for the spike fixture and added dynamic while/range negative tests by rejecting loop constructs that need a real execution budget before eval
required_scenarios.fail_closed_on_sandbox_import_filesystem: passed for visible load/import plus explicit filesystem/network attempt tests

checks.command: mise trust /Users/admin/projects/ingary.starlark-elixir-rustler/mise.toml && mise exec -- mix deps.get
checks.notes: Trusted the repo mise config, resolved rustler 0.36.2 and toml 0.7.0 in addition to existing deps.

latency_and_safety_observations: Full ExUnit suite with release NIF compile completed in about 2 seconds after dependencies were built; endpoint test logged 8-11 ms for a simple policy request in test mode.
The Starlark Rust crate is a large dependency graph, which is material for compile time and audit surface.
Dirty CPU scheduling is the right BEAM integration shape for this spike, but production needs a real execution budget rather than static source rejection.
limitations: No general Starlark fuel, deadline, or killable execution boundary is implemented. The spike fail-closes `while` and `range()` loop constructs before eval, which is conservative and testable but too restrictive for production policy authoring.

Context is injected by rendering JSON as a Starlark literal and appending an evaluate(ctx) call. This is deterministic for JSON-compatible ABI values, but a production bridge should prefer typed allocation or a frozen module/cache strategy.
Sandbox checks are conservative string prechecks plus no module loader; they are not a complete static security analysis.
Source span metadata is not emitted.
No production receipt integration was added; this is a prototype-only callable test surface.

viability_verdict.summary: Rustler can expose a deterministic Starlark evaluator to the Elixir backend with clean fail-closed tests and a simple endpoint. It is not production-viable until arbitrary expensive policies have a real interruptible budget, preferably through a sidecar/process boundary or confirmed runtime fuel support.

notes: Blocker found: initial runaway defense only rejected obvious while True / while 1 loops, leaving dynamic while conditions and huge range loops able to occupy a dirty scheduler indefinitely.
Follow-up fix: reject all while loops and range() loops before native evaluation, and add ExUnit plus Rust native negative tests for dynamic while and expensive range policies.
Remaining architecture concern: static loop rejection is acceptable for a spike but not a production substitute for runtime fuel, deadline, or a killable process boundary.
Test quality review: tests exercise observable ABI behavior through the Elixir callable and endpoint, include fail-closed negative cases, and now cover non-vacuous dynamic runaway patterns.
